abstract: This paper applies linear concept erasure (LEACE) to pretrained text embeddings to remove information about observed confounders—such as document source or language—that bias similarity-based analyses. The authors formalize how erasure removes confounding signal from document similarity estimands, construct a benchmark of paired multi-source and multilingual datasets, and evaluate ten embedding models on clustering and retrieval tasks. They find that erasure substantially improves performance (e.g., multilingual Recall@1 from 0.175 to 0.826) without degrading out-of-distribution benchmark results, suggesting the method is a low-cost, broadly useful preprocessing step.
key_claims: Pretrained sentence embeddings encode spurious attributes (source, language, style) that act as observed confounders and bias document similarity measures.
Applying LEACE to embeddings can be cast as removing the loading on observed confounders in a structural decomposition of similarity, purging that contribution from dot-product similarity.
Linear erasure of confounder subspaces consistently improves clustering and similarity-search quality across embedding models, datasets, and metrics.
Erasure trained on one domain does not degrade performance on unrelated out-of-distribution MTEB retrieval and STS tasks, indicating embeddings are not otherwise harmed.
LEACE gains are largest when the targeted confounder aligns with dominant variance directions (e.g., PC1) in the original embedding space.
For applied practitioners pooling texts from heterogeneous sources or languages, linear erasure should be a default preprocessing step.
methods: Adapt the LEACE closed-form linear concept erasure algorithm (Belrose et al., 2023) to remove subspaces predictive of confounding metadata (source, language) from precomputed embeddings.

Construct a benchmark with category-level data (Comparative Agendas Project: bills, executive orders, newspapers) and event-level paired data (Super-SCOTUS from Wikipedia/LexisNexis/Oyez; SemEval 2022 Task 8 multilingual news; SwilTra-Bench Swiss court summaries in German/French/Italian).
Evaluate ten embedding models (MiniLM, GIST-small/base/large, multilingual E5-small/base/large, MPNet, Nomic-v2, MXB-large) varying in size, dimensionality, multilinguality, and instruction tuning.
Measure clustering quality with k-means purity and Adjusted Rand Index (k=21), and retrieval quality with Recall@1 and Recall@10 on paired items pooled with unpaired distractors.
Test out-of-distribution effects by applying erasers trained on CAP and Legal (LexisNexis–Wikipedia) data to MTEB legal retrieval, news retrieval, and STS news tasks using MiniLM and E5-base-v2.
Compare LEACE to a PCA baseline that removes the first principal component, and analyze the correlation between PC1 variance and Recall@1 improvement.
Additional bitext mining experiments on 28 MTEB tasks with E5-large-instruct, and qualitative analysis of legal case summary pairs.
findings: Across all four CAP source pairings and all ten models, erasing source information improved purity and ARI (e.g., GIST-small on Bills–Newspapers gained +0.169 purity and +0.157 ARI).

On Swiss court summaries, LEACE produced dramatic gains for cross-family languages: E5-large Recall@1 rose by +0.651 on DE–IT and E5-base by +0.570 on DE–FR; PCA showed the first principal component before erasure separated languages almost perfectly.
On SCOTUS paired summaries, every model and dataset combination improved with erasure, with E5-small gaining +0.177 Recall@1 on LexisNexis–Wikipedia.
All ten models improved on SemEval multilingual news retrieval after erasing language (e.g., E5-small +0.236 Recall@1).
Applying CAP- or Legal-trained erasers to MTEB legal retrieval, news retrieval, and STS tasks left performance essentially unchanged, with no meaningful degradation and occasional small gains.
The proportion of variance explained by PC1 in original embeddings correlated strongly with the Recall@1 improvement from LEACE (r = 0.79, p < 0.001).
A naive PCA baseline (removing PC1) yielded inconsistent in-domain gains and catastrophically degraded MTEB performance, unlike LEACE.
On MTEB bitext mining with E5-large-instruct, LEACE achieved state-of-the-art on three public leaderboard tasks, with no task degrading by more than 0.01 F1.

Erasure is less effective when confounder categories are very numerous relative to data size (e.g., within-language SemEval news with many sources) and may fail in some short-query retrieval settings (Huang et al., 2024).
contributions: A formal account of how linear concept erasure removes observed-confounder contributions from dot-product document similarity via a structural decomposition.
A new benchmark of paired category-level (CAP) and event-level (SCOTUS, SemEval, Swiss courts) datasets designed to measure confounder effects on embedding-based tasks.
Extensive empirical evidence across ten embedding models that LEACE consistently improves clustering and retrieval quality, often dramatically, without harming out-of-distribution MTEB performance.
Diagnostic analysis linking LEACE's effectiveness to the alignment of confounders with dominant variance (PC1) directions in embedding space.
Practical guidance and open-source code for applying linear erasure as a cheap preprocessing step in computational social science and other applied settings working with heterogeneous corpora.
framing: The paper sits at the intersection of representation debiasing/concept erasure (Bolukbasi et al., 2016; Ravfogel et al., 2020, 2022; Belrose et al., 2023) and applied uses of sentence embeddings (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019) in computational social science and document analysis. It reframes longstanding concerns about corpus-driven structure in unsupervised text analysis (Thompson and Mimno, 2018) as a problem of observed confounding in embedding similarity, and argues that closed-form linear erasure is a principled, low-cost remedy for practitioners pooling documents across sources and languages.
